title: Understanding reduction atmosphere in kilns
subtitle: How oxygen deficiency in a kiln changes glaze and clay body colour
excerpt: Reduction atmosphere in a kiln means that the available oxygen is insufficient to fully combust the fuel or is chemically drawn from metal oxides in the glaze. This changes the colour of iron and copper glazes dramatically and can produce effects that are not achievable in an electric oxidising kiln.
sourceNotes: Reduction firing principles derived from first principles and general ceramic science.

glossaryTerms.definition: A kiln atmosphere with insufficient oxygen to fully combust the fuel. Reduction withdraws oxygen from metal oxides in glazes and clay bodies, changing their colour.
A kiln atmosphere with sufficient oxygen for complete combustion. Standard electric kilns fire in oxidation. Most commercial glazes are formulated for oxidation firing.
A mineral pigment used in ceramics. Under oxidation, iron produces amber-brown tones. Under reduction, iron produces grey-green celadon and blue-grey tones.
A refractory clay box placed in the kiln to contain combustible materials packed around the ceramics, creating a localized reduction atmosphere within an otherwise oxidising electric kiln.

content.text: Reduction
and
oxidation

describe the availability of oxygen in the kiln during firing. All standard electric kilns fire in oxidation. Gas and wood kilns can be fired in reduction. The atmosphere at firing temperature determines the colour of
iron oxide
and copper oxide in glazes. These effects cannot be reproduced in an electric kiln without special techniques.

attrs.content: Reduction firing, saggar firing, and gas kiln work all produce carbon monoxide and combustion gases. These processes must be carried out with proper ventilation and appropriate fire safety measures. Never fire a gas kiln or a sawdust saggar in an enclosed space.

content.text: How to access reduction effects
Use glazes with iron or copper in them. These give the strongest colour changes under reduction.
Use a community gas kiln, saggar firing in an electric kiln, or raku for reduction at home.
Load your pieces and request a reduction atmosphere from the firing schedule.
Open the kiln only after it has cooled below 200°C and inspect the results.
What reduction does to glazes

Iron oxide in oxidation gives amber and rust tones. In reduction, the same iron gives grey-green celadon and blue-grey tones. Copper oxide in oxidation gives greens. In reduction, copper gives the deep ox-blood red known as copper red. This happens because the kiln atmosphere draws oxygen from the metal oxides, changing their colour.
What reduction does to clay bodies

The clay body is also affected by the kiln atmosphere. Iron in the clay (especially in red earthenware or iron-bearing stoneware) shifts from rusty red in oxidation to grey-brown or near-black in reduction. Flash marks are dark patches where the flame path contacts the clay directly. They give wood-fired and gas-fired pots their characteristic irregular look.
Accessing reduction as a home ceramicist
Most home ceramicists work with an electric kiln, which fires in oxidation. Several approaches give access to reduction effects:
Community studios: many let members book space in a gas kiln for reduction firing, charged per shelf.
Saggar

firing in an electric kiln: pack the bisqued piece in a saggar with combustibles such as sawdust, salt, or steel wool. The saggar creates a local reduction zone. Results are more subtle than a gas reduction, but you get carbon flash and surface variation.
Raku: the post-firing reduction step in raku firing produces localised reduction effects on the surface. See the raku firing tutorial for full details.
Choosing glazes for reduction

Not all glazes respond dramatically to reduction. The most interesting reduction effects are in glazes with significant iron or copper content. A clear glaze on reduction-fired stoneware produces a warmer, slightly greyer tone than the same glaze in oxidation. Ask specifically for glazes formulated or tested for reduction when sourcing materials for gas-kiln firing.

items.symptom: Electric kiln glaze results look flat compared to gas-fired examples
items.cause: Electric kilns fire in oxidation. The colour changes that come from reduction are not possible in an electric kiln
items.fix: For oxidation, choose glazes specifically designed for electric kilns. Seek a community studio with a gas kiln for occasional reduction firings

items.symptom: Saggar firing produced only surface smudging, not clear reduction effects
items.cause: Not enough combustible material was in the saggar. The reduction atmosphere was not strong enough to hold
items.fix: Fill the saggar more densely with combustible material and ensure it is sealed well. Results improve with experimentation
